narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—just over a 51% chance of not passing. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: the strongest signal was your Year 3 decile of 2, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers, which painted a picture of challenges ramping up later in school and strongly tipped the scales toward predicting failure. Layered on that, your LSAT score of 32 stood out as another heavy anchor, signaling to the model that the raw analytical firepower needed for bar success might be a stretch. Your Year 1 decile of 2 echoed that early pattern, suggesting consistency in those lower rankings rather than a climb. Even with a middle-family income, which doesn't offer the extra cushions some students have, it nudged things further toward caution. Your Asian background and undergraduate GPA of 2.4 added to the mosaic, as the model has seen similar profiles struggle more often with the bar's demands. Going full-time, while ambitious, didn't counterbalance these, nor did being male in this context—everything aligned to tell a story of hurdles ahead. It's not that you're incapable; it's that these pieces together made the model see echoes of past students who faced similar odds and didn't make it across the finish line. The good news? You're holding some cards like your drive to target law school—use this as fuel to boost those grades or LSAT, and rewrite your story for the next chapter. Hang in there; many have turned profiles like yours around.
